Reported effort put into the test after the cognitive assessment. Index variable summarizing student responses.
Reported effort the student would have invested after the cognitive assessment. Index variable.
ISCO-08 occupation code for the mother. Aggregate variable summarizing parental occupations.
ISCO-08 occupation code for the mother. Aggregate variable summarizing parental occupations.
ISCO-08 occupation code for the student (if applicable). Aggregate variable.
Unique national study program code. Aggregate variable specific to national context.
Student's age in years. Aggregate demographic variable.
Grade compared to the modal grade in the country. Index variable calculated from school data.
Levels of education programs according to ISCED 2011. Aggregate variable reflecting international education standards.
Index indicating the student's immigrant background (OECD definition): : 1 = Native, 2 = Second-Generation, 3 = First-Generation, 9 = Missing
The student's country of birth. Aggregate demographic variable.
The mother's country of birth. Aggregate demographic variable.
The father's country of birth. Aggregate demographic variable.

Language spoken at home by the student: Central Thai (555), regional Thai dialects (616) (e.g., Isan, Northern Thai, Southern Thai), non-Thai languages (855) (e.g., Burmese, Khmer, Chinese dialects, Malay), or missing (999).
Whether the student has repeated a grade. Index variable reflecting academic progression. 0 = never repeated a level, 1 = repeated one or more levels, 9 = missing

Whether the student has missed school for more than three months. Aggregate variable summarizing absence trends. 0 = never missed schooll for more than three months, 1 = missed school for more than three months at least once, 9 = missing

Whether the student skips classes or days of school. Index variable reflecting attendance issues. 0 = never skipped school in the last two weeks, 1 = skipped school at least once in the last two weeks, 9 = missing
Arriving late for school under a stricter definition. Index variable capturing punctuality issues. 0 = never late, 1 = occasionally late, 2 = frequently late, 9 = missing

Participation in sports or physical activity before or after school. Aggregate variable summarizing extracurricular activity. ("0 days", "1 day", "2 days", "3 days", "4 days", "5 or more days"). Values on this index range from 0 (no exercise or sports) to 10 (10 or more times exercise or sport a per week), 99 = missing

Studying or doing homework before or after school. Aggregate variable summarizing study habits. ("0 days", "1 day", "2 days", "3 days", "4 days", "5 or more days"). Values on this index range from 0 (no studying) to 10 (10 or more times of studying per week). 99 = Missing

Working for pay before or after school. Aggregate variable reflecting economic activity. ("0 days", "1 day", "2 days", "3 days", "4 days", "5 or more days"). Values on this index range from 0 (no work for pay) to 10 (10 or more times of working for pay per week). 99 = Missing

Working in the household or taking care of family members before or after school. Aggregate variable reflecting familial responsibilities. ("0 days", "1 day", "2 days", "3 days", "4 days", "5 or more days"). Values on this index range from 0 (no work in household or care of family members) to 10 (10 or more times of working in household or caring for family members per week). 99 = Missing
The student's highest expected educational level. Index variable summarizing aspirations.
Preference for mathematics over other core subjects. WLE variable measuring subject preferences.
The perception that mathematics is easier than other subjects. WLE variable reflecting ease of learning.
The student's motivation to do well in mathematics. WLE variable quantifying drive.
Duration of participation in early childhood education and care programs. Aggregate variable summarizing early learning experiences.
Student's expected occupation status (free response, four-digit code). Index variable summarizing occupational aspirations.

Whether the student has a clear idea about their future job. Index variable summarizing career clarity. Students who had no clear idea about their future jobs were considered those who indicated "I do not know" or gave a vague answer such as "a good job", "a quiet job", "a well-paid job", "an office job" in response to question ST329. In the OCOD3 index, "I don't know" responses were coded 9704 and vague responses were coded 9705. Examples of invalid responses include students who did not answer the question or gave an answer, such as a smiley face. Specifically, a value of "0" is assigned on the index if OCOD3 values are 9704 or 9705, and a value of "1" is assigned if OCOD3 values are 0000 to 9703.

Quality of the student-teacher relationships. Derived using WLE to reflect latent perceptions. Students' ratings of their agreement with the eight statements (e.g., "The teachers at my school are respectful towards me.", "When my teachers ask how I am doing, they are really interested in my answer.") in question ST267 were scaled into the index of "Quality of student-teacher relationships". Note that this scale used a within-construct matrix sampling design. Each of the eight items included in this scale had four response options ("Strongly disagree", "Disagree", "Agree", "Strongly agree").
Sense of belonging at school. WLE variable measuring inclusion.
Experiences of being bullied. WLE variable summarizing peer interactions.
Feeling safe at school. WLE variable capturing perceptions of safety.
Risks to safety at school. WLE variable summarizing latent risks.
Agreement with perseverance statements. WLE variable reflecting persistence traits.
Agreement with perseverance statements. self-constructed derived variable reflecting persistence traits.
Agreement with curiosity statements. WLE variable summarizing inquisitiveness.
Agreement with cooperation statements. WLE variable measuring collaboration tendencies.
Agreement with empathy statements. WLE variable summarizing emotional understanding.
Agreement with assertiveness statements. WLE variable reflecting confidence.
Agreement with stress resistance statements. WLE variable measuring resilience.
Agreement with emotional control statements. WLE variable summarizing emotional regulation.
Seeking information about future careers. WLE variable reflecting career planning.

Family support for the student. Students’ ratings of how often their parents or someone else in their family engaged in a range of behaviours indicative of family support (e.g., “Discuss how well you are doing at school”, “Spend time just talking with you”) in question ST300 were scaled into the index of “Family support”.
The disciplinary climate in mathematics classes. WLE variable summarizing classroom behavior.
Teacher support in mathematics classes. WLE variable quantifying academic support.
Cognitive activation in mathematics: fostering reasoning. WLE variable reflecting critical thinking.
Cognitive activation in mathematics: encouraging mathematical thinking. WLE variable measuring problem-solving.
Exposure to formal and applied mathematics tasks. WLE variable reflecting curriculum depth.
Exposure to mathematical reasoning and 21st-century mathematics tasks. WLE variable summarizing modern competencies.
Mathematics self-efficacy for formal and applied tasks, with reversed response options in 2022. WLE variable reflecting confidence.
Mathematics self-efficacy for reasoning and 21st-century skills. WLE variable reflecting skills.
Subjective familiarity with mathematics concepts. WLE variable measuring understanding.
Mathematics anxiety, measuring students' emotional responses to math. Derived using Weighted Likelihood Estimates (WLE).
Effort and persistence in mathematics, capturing students' commitment to math learning. Calculated using WLE.
School actions or activities aimed at sustaining learning during school closures due to Covid-19, inlcuding the use of ICT. An Index/Proportion variable summarizing multiple responses.
Types of learning resources, including digital materials, used while the school was closed. An Index/Proportion variable summarizing resource availability.
Problems, including ICT problems, faced in self-directed learning. Calculated using WLE to represent latent traits.
Family support for self-directed learning, including the use of ICT. Derived using WLE to quantify parental involvement.
Index scale measuring feelings about learning at home during school closures due to Covid-19. A WLE variable summarizing emotional responses.
Self-directed learning self-efficacy, including the use of ICT, capturing confidence in managing learning independently should their school building close again in the future. Derived using WLE.
Mother's level of education based on ISCED classification. An Index/Proportion variable standardized for international comparison.
Father's level of education based on ISCED classification. An Index/Proportion variable.
Highest level of education of parents based on ISCED classification. Combines individual parent education into an Index/Proportion.
Index of parents' highest education level in international years of schooling. Combines parent responses into a standardized Index/Proportion.
Mother's occupational status based on ISCO 4-digit classification. An Index/Proportion variable indicating social status.
Father's occupational status based on ISCO 4-digit classification. A proportion-based variable reflecting occupational prestige.
Highest parental occupational status based on ISCO 4-digit classification. An Index summarizing parental career achievements.

Availability of ICT resources in the student's home environment. Calculated as a WLE to reflect access across multiple items. Students reported on the availability of 11 Information and Communications Technologies (ICT) resources in their home (e.g., "A computer (laptop, desktop, or tablet) that you can use for school work", "Internet access (e.g., Wi-fi) (excluding through smartphones)") in questions ST250 (which had two response categories), ST253 (which had eight response categories), and ST254 (which had four substantive response categories and an additional response category "I don't know," which was recoded as missing prior to scaling). These items were scaled into the index of "ICT resources". Table 19.17
Measure of possessions and resources available at home. An Index/Proportion variable summarizing material wealth and cultural capital.
Economic, social, and cultural status index. Combines family background and possessions into a standardized Index/Proportion.

ICT availability and use at school. A WLE variable derived from school-level data. Students' frequency ratings of how often they use various digital resources at school (e.g., "Desktop or laptop computer", "Smartphone (i.e., mobile phone with internet access)") in question IC170 were scaled into the index of "ICT availability at school". Each of the seven items included in this scale had six response options ("Never or almost never", "About once or twice a month", "About once or twice a week", "Every day or almost every day", "Several times a day", "This resource is not available to me at school").

Availability and usage of ICT at school. Derived from WLE to reflect standardized ICT resources. The index was calculated as the number of all seven items that were marked with a value other than "This resource is not available to me at school"), thus ranging from 0-7.

ICT availability and use outside school. A WLE summarizing access to ICT in home settings. Students' frequency ratings of how often they use various digital resources outside of school (e.g., "Desktop or laptop computer", "Smartphone (i.e., mobile phone with internet access)") in question IC171 were scaled into the index of "ICT availability outside school". Each of the six items included in this scale had six response options ("Never or almost never", "About once or twice a month", "About once or twice a week", "Every day or almost every day", "Several times a day",

Availability and usage of ICT at home. Calculated using WLE to represent students' digital access. The availability of ICT outside of school was gathered from IC171 where students' frequency ratings of how often they use various digital resources outside of school (e.g., "Desktop or laptop computer", "Smartphone") was used for the index of "ICT use outside of school". Each of the six items in this question included six response options ("Never or almost never", "About once or twice a month", "About once or twice a week", "Every day or almost every day", "Several times a day", "This resource is not available to me outside of school").

Quality of ICT access at school as perceived by students. A WLE variable summarizing access and usability perceptions. Students’ ratings of their agreement with various statements about ICT resources at their school (e.g., “There are enough digital devices with access to the Internet at my school.”, “The school’s Internet speed is sufficient.”) in question IC172 were scaled into the index of “Quality of access to ICT”. Each of the nine items included in this scale had four response options (“Strongly disagree”, “Disagree”, “Agree”, “Strongly agree”).

Use of ICT in subject-related lessons. Derived using WLE to quantify technology integration in classrooms. Students’ frequency ratings of how often digital resources are used in various subject lessons (e.g., “Mathematics”, “Science”) in question IC173 were scaled into the index of “Subject-related ICT use during lessons”. Each of the four items included in this scale had five substantive response options (“Never or almost never, “In less than half of the lessons”, “In about half of the lessons”, “In more than half of the lessons”, “In every or almost every lesson”) and an additional response option “I do not have this subject” which was recoded as missing prior to scaling.
Use of ICT in school-based learning activities. Calculated using WLE to represent active learning practices.
Use of ICT in school-based learning activities. Self-calculated using WLE to represent active learning practices.
Support or feedback provided by teachers via ICT. A WLE variable summarizing teacher or peer support through technology.
Support or feedback provided by teachers via ICT. Self-calculated WLE variable summarizing teacher or peer support through technology.
Students' frequency ratings of how often they use digital resources for various school-related activities outside of the classroom. Derived using WLE.
Students' frequency ratings of how often they use digital resources for various school-related activities outside of the classroom. Derived using WLE.

Frequency of ICT activity for leisure on weekdays. A WLE variable summarizing digital activity patterns. Students’ frequency ratings of how often they did various leisure activities using ICT during a typical week day (e.g., “Play video-games (using my smartphone, a gaming console or an online platform or apps)”, “Look for practical information online (e.g., find a place, book a train ticket, buy a product)”) in question IC177 were scaled into the index of “Frequency of ICT activity – Weekday”. Each of the seven items included in this scale had six response options (“No time at all, “Less than 1 hour a day”, “Between 1 and 3 hours a day”, “More than 3 hours and up to 5 hours a day”, “More than 5 hours and up to 7 hours a day”, “More than 7 hours a day”).

Frequency of ICT activity for leisure on weekends. Calculated as a WLE to reflect students' technology habits. Students’ frequency ratings of how often they did various leisure activities using ICT during a typicalweekend day (e.g., “Play video-games (using my smartphone, a gaming console or an online platform orapps)”, “Look for practical information online (e.g., find a place, book a train ticket, buy aproduct)”) in question IC178 were scaled into the index of “Frequency of ICT activity – Weekend”. Each ofthe seven items included in this scale had six response options (“No time at all, “Less than 1 hour a day”,“Between 1 and 3 hours a day”, “More than 3 hours and up to 5 hours a day”, “More than 5 hours and upto 7 hours a day”, “More than 7 hours a day”).

Perceptions of regulated ICT use in school. A WLE variable summarizing structured ICT policies. Students' ratings of their agreement with various statements about regulation of ICT use at school (e.g., "Students should not be allowed to bring mobile phones to class.", "The school should set up filters to prevent students from playing games online.") in question IC179 were scaled into the index of "Views of regulated ICT use in school". Each of the six items included in this scale had four response options ("Strongly disagree", "Disagree", "Agree", "Strongly agree").

Students' practices regarding online information use. A WLE summarizing internet behaviors. Students' ratings of their agreement with various statements about their practices regarding online information (e.g., "When searching for information online I compare different sources.", "I discuss the accuracy of online information with friends or other students.") in question IC180 were scaled into the index of "Students' practices regarding online information".

Distress caused by online content, including cyberbullying. A WLE variable measuring emotional impacts. Students' ratings of how upset they were when various situation occurred online (e.g., "Encountering content online that was inappropriate for my age", "Receiving unkind, vulgar or offending messages, comments or videos") in question IC181 were scaled into the index of "Distress from online content and cyberbullying".

Self-efficacy in digital competencies. Derived using WLE to reflect confidence in technology skills. Students' ratings of how well they can do various tasks using digital resources (e.g., "Search for and find relevant information online", "Write or edit text for a school assignment") in question IC183 were scaled into the index of "Self-efficacy in digital competencies". Each of the 14 items included in this scale had four substantive response options ("I cannot do this", "I struggle to do this on my own", "I can do with a bit of effort", "I can easily do this") and an additional response option "I don't know what this is" which was recoded as missing prior to scaling.

Principals were asked in SC012 about admittance policies at their school, including student academicperformance and recommendation by feeder schools. The three response categories for this question were“Never”, “Sometimes”, and “Always”.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for school autonomy. Principals were asked in SC202 about who had the main responsibility for various decisions or activities at their school (e.g., “Appointing or hiring teachers”, “Determining teachers’ salary increases”). The six response categories for the 12 items in the scale were “Principal”, “Teachers or members of <school management team>”, “<School governing board>”, “<Local or municipal authority>”, “<Regional or state authority>”, and “<National or federal authority>”. Higher scale score values indicate that the principal,teachers or members of the school management team, and the school governing board had a greater levelof autonomy in decision-making activities at their school. Lower scale score values indicate that thesegroups had less autonomy.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for teacher participation. Principals were asked in SC202 about who had the main responsibility for various decisions or activities at their school (e.g., “Formulating the school budget”, “Choosing which learning materials are used”). The six response categories for the 12 items in the scale were “Principal”, “Teachers or members of <school management team>”, “<School governing board>”, “<Local or municipal authority>”, “<Regional or state authority>”, and “<National or federal authority>”. Higher scale score values indicate that the teachers or members of the school management team participated to a greater extent in decision-making activities at their school. Lower scale score values indicate that they participated to a lesser extent.

School responsibility for curriculum. Principals were asked in SC202 about who had the main responsibility for various decisions or activities at their school. The six response categories for this question were “Principal”, “Teachers or members of <school management team>”, “<School governing board>”, “<Local or municipal authority>”, “<Regional or state authority>”, and “<National or federal authority>”. An index of the relative level of responsibility of school staff in deciding issues related to curriculum and assessment (RESPCUR) was computed from the school principals’ reports regarding who had the main responsibility for 4 items in SC202. The index was calculated on the basis of the ratio of responses for “Principal”, “Teachers or members of <school management team>”, or “<School governing board>” on the one hand to responses for “<Local or municipal authority>”, “<Regional or state authority>”, or “<National or federal authority>” on the other hand. In the first step, a count for school responsibility was calculated by counting the number of “Principal”, “Teachers or members of <school management team>”, and “<School governing board>” responses. In the second step, a count for non-school responsibility was calculated by counting the number of “<Local or municipal authority>”, “<Regional or state authority>”, and “<National or federal authority>”. In the third step, the school responsibility count was divided by the non-school responsibility count. To avoid dividing by ”0”, “1” was added to both the numerator and denominator; when the ratio of school responsibility to non-school responsibility was 4:0, an index value of 4 was assigned. Higher values indicated relatively higher levels of school responsibility in deciding issues related to curriculum and assessment.

School responsibility for resources. Principals were asked in SC202 about who had the main responsibility for various decisions or activities at their school. The six response categories for this question were “Principal”, “Teachers or members of <school management team>”, “<School governing board>”, “<Local or municipal authority>”, “<Regional or state authority>”, and “<National or federal authority>”. An index of the relative level of responsibility of school staff in deciding issues related to allocating resources (RESPRES) was computed from the school principals’ reports regarding who had the main responsibility for 6 items in SC202. The index was calculated on the basis of the ratio of responses for “Principal”, “Teachers or members of <school management team>”, or “<School governing board>” on the one hand to responses for “<Local or municipal authority>”, “<Regional or state authority>”, or “<National or federal authority>” on the other hand. In the first step, a count for school responsibility was calculated by counting the number of “Principal”, “Teachers or members of <school management team>”, and “<School governing board>” responses. In the second step, a count for non-school responsibility was calculated by counting the number of “<Local or municipal authority>”, “<Regional or state authority>”, and “<National or federal authority>”. In the third step, the school responsibility count was divided by the non-school responsibility count. To avoid dividing by ”0”, “1” was added to both the numerator and denominator; when the ratio of school responsibility to non-school responsibility was 6:0, an index value of 6 was assigned. Higher values on the scale indicated relatively higher levels of school responsibility in this area.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for educational leadership. Principals were asked in SC201 about how often they or other members of their school management teamengaged in activities or behaviours related to educational leadership during the past 12 months(e.g., “Collaborating with teachers to solve classroom discipline problems”, “Providing parents or guardianswith information on the school and student performance”).

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for instructional leadership. Principals were asked in SC201 about how often they or other members of their school management teamengaged in activities or behaviours related to teaching or instructional leadership during the last 12 months(e.g., “Providing feedback to teachers based on observations of instruction in the classroom”, “Takingactions to ensure that teachers feel responsible for their students' learning outcomes”).

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for school encouragement of parent/guardian involvement. Principals were asked in SC192 about how often their school staff engaged parents or guardians in variousaspects of students’ educational environment during the last academic year (e.g., “Invited parents orguardians to volunteer for school activities”, “Initiated communications with parents or guardians about their child’s progress”).

Availability of computers. School principals were asked in SC004 to report the number of digital devices available for 15-year-oldstudents at their school. The index of availability of computers (RATCMP1) is the ratio of the number of desktop or laptop computers available for these students for educational purposes (SC004Q02TA) to thetotal number of students in the modal grade for 15-year-olds at their school (SC004Q01TA).

Computers connected to the Internet. School principals were asked in SC004 to report the number of desktop or laptop computers at their schoolthat are connected to the Internet. The index of computers connected to the Internet (RATCMP2) is theratio of the number of desktop or laptop computers available for 15-year-olds for educational purposes.

Availability of tablet devices. School principals were asked in SC004 to report the number of tablet devices or e-book readers availablefor 15-year-old students at their school for educational purposes (SC004Q08JA). The index of availabilityof tablet devices (RATTAB) is the ratio of the number of tablet devices available for these students foreducational purposes to the total number of students in the modal grade for 15-year-olds at their school(SC004Q01TA).

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for digital device policies at the school. School principals were asked in SC190 to indicate whether their school had various policies regarding digital device use (e.g., "Teachers establish rules for when students may use digital devices during lessons.", "The school has a specific programme to prepare students for responsible internet behaviour."). The two response categories for the nine items in the scale were "Yes" and "No".

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for digital device policies at the school. School principals were asked in SC190 to indicate whether their school had various policies regarding digital device use (e.g., "Teachers establish rules for when students may use digital devices during lessons.", "The school has a specific programme to prepare students for responsible internet behaviour."). The two response categories for the nine items in the scale were "Yes" and "No".

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for feedback to teachers. Principals were asked in SC193 how much impact teacher evaluations had on various matters (e.g., "A change in salary", "A change in the likelihood of career advancement").

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for mathematics teacher training. School principals were asked in SC184 to indicate the areas in which professional development was offered to mathematics teachers in their school (e.g., "Mathematics content", "Mathematics curriculum").

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for school diversity and multicultural views. Principals were asked in SC017 about the extent to which instruction is hindered by a shortage of educational materials in their school.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for school diversity and multicultural views. Principals were asked in SC017 about the extent to which instruction is hindered by a shortage of educational materials in their school.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for negative school climate. Principals were asked in SC172 about the extent of problem behaviours that contribute to a negative school climate in their school. Higher scale score values indicate that problem behaviours contribute to a negative school climate to a greater extent, while lower scale score values indicate that problem behaviours impact school climate to a lesser extent.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for shortage of educational staff. Principals were asked in SC017 about the extent to which instruction is hindered by a shortage of educational staff in their school (e.g., “A lack of teaching staff”, “Inadequate or poorly qualified assisting staff”). Higher scale score values indicate that the school is impacted by a shortage of educational staff to a greater extent, while lower scale score values indicate that the school is impacted to a lesser extent by a shortage of educational staff.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for shortage of educational material. Principals were asked in SC017 about the extent to which instruction is hindered by a shortage of educational materials in their school (e.g., “A lack of educational material (e.g., textbooks, IT equipment, library or laboratory material)”, “Inadequate or poor quality educational material (e.g. textbooks, IT equipment, library or laboratory material)”). Higher scale score values indicate that the school is impacted by a shortage of educational materials to a greater extent, while lower scale score values indicate that the school is impacted to a lesser extent by a shortage of educational materials.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for student-related factors affecting school climate. Principals were asked in SC061 about the extent to which student learning is hindered by student behaviours (e.g., “Student truancy”, “Student use of alcohol or illegal drugs”). Note that this scale was linked to the STUBEHA scale in PISA 2018. The four response categories for the six items in the scale were “Not at all”, “Very little”, “To some extent”, and “A lot”. Higher scale score values indicate that students’ learning is hindered to a greater extent by negative student behaviours, while lower scale score values indicate that students’ learning is hindered by negative student behaviours to a lesser extent.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for teacher-related factors affecting school climate. Principals were asked in SC061 about the extent to which student learning is hindered by teacherbehaviours (e.g., “Teacher absenteeism”, “Staff resisting change”). The four response categories for thefive items in the scale were “Not at all”, “Very little”, “To some extent”, and “A lot”. Higher scale score values indicate that students’ learning is hindered to a greater extent by negative teacher behaviours, while lowerscale score values indicate that students’ learning is hindered by negative teacher behaviours to a lesserextent.

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for use of standardized tests. Principals were asked in SC035 to indicate whether standardised tests were used for various purposes(e.g., “To guide students’ learning”, “To group students for instructional purposes”).

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for use of teacher-developed tests. Principals were asked in SC035 to indicate whether standardised tests were used for various purposes(e.g., “To guide students’ learning”, “To group students for instructional purposes”).

Mathematics-related extra-curricular activities at school. School principals were asked in SC053 to report what mathematics-related extra-curricular activities theirschools offered to 15-year-old students. The two response categories were “Yes” and “No”.
Mathematics extension courses offered at the school. School principals were asked in SC181 to report what additional mathematics lessons are offered at theirschool.
Ability grouping for mathematics classes. School principals were asked in SC187 to report the extent to which their mathematics classes catered tostudents with different abilities.

School Closure Support from Education Authorities. School administrators who indicated that their school was closed for one ormore school days because of COVID-19 were asked to rate the extent that they felt their school wassupported by educational authorities during the time that their school building was closed to studentsbecause of COVID-19. They reported this information by selecting one of four response options: “Not atall”; “Very little”; “To some extent”; “A lot”. Respondents may interpret support broadly to include any kindof assistance (i.e., financial support, volunteer support, etc).

School Closure Support from Other Sources. School administrators who indicated that their school was closed for one or moreschool days because of COVID-19 were asked to rate the extent that they felt their school was supportedby students’ parents or guardians and by private donors during the time that their school building wasclosed to students because of COVID-19. They reported this information by selecting one of four responseoptions: “Not at all”; “Very little”; “To some extent”; “A lot”. Respondents may interpret support broadly toinclude any kind of assistance (i.e., financial support, volunteer support, etc).

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for problems with schools' capacity to provide remote instruction. School administrators were asked in SC216 to what extent specific challenges hindered their school's capacity to provide remote instruction during the time when the school building was closed to students because of COVID-19 (e.g., "Lack of access to <digital devices> among students", "Lack of learning management systems or school learning platforms (e.g., [Blackboard¬Æ], [Edmodo¬Æ], [Moodle¬Æ], [Google¬Æ Classroom‚Ñ¢])"). The four response categories for the five items in the scale were "Not at all", "Very little", "To some extent", and "A lot".

School preparation for remote instruction before the pandemic. School administrators were asked in SC223 whether their school had taken specific actions to prepare forremote instruction (e.g., “Adapting existing curriculum plans for remote instruction (e.g., modifying courserequirements, sequence of lessons, grading policies)”, “Ensuring that students have access to <digitaldevices> for remote instruction”).

School preparation for remote instruction in response to the pandemic. School administrators were asked in SC223 whether their school had taken specific actions to prepare forremote instruction (e.g., “Adapting existing curriculum plans for remote instruction (e.g., modifying courserequirements, sequence of lessons, grading policies)”, “Ensuring that students have access to <digitaldevices> for remote instruction”).

Weighted Likelihood Estimate (WLE) for preparedness for digital learning. School administrators were asked in SC155 to rate their agreement with statements about their school’s capacity to use digital devices to enhance learning and teaching (“Teachers have sufficient time to prepare lessons integrating digital devices”, “The school has sufficient qualified technical assistant staff”). The four response categories for the six items in the scale were “Strongly disagree”, “Disagree”, “Agree”, and “Strongly agree”. Higher scale score values indicate greater capacity for a school to use digitaltechnology for learning and teaching, while lower scale score values indicate less capacity for a school todo so.

Grade Nonresponse Adjusted School Base Weight: A weight applied to schools, adjusted for nonresponse in specific grades. This weight ensures that data from sampled schools accurately represents the target population by accounting for grade-level nonresponse.

Sum of Final Student Weights at School Level: The sum of all student weights (W_FSTUWT) within a school. This aggregated weight is used in analyses that require school-level weighted statistics to account for the complex survey sampling design.

Count of Students with Final Student Weights at School Level: The total number of students in a school who have a valid final student weight (W_FSTUWT). This variable is used to determine the number of students contributing to the weighted school-level data.
